essayHeadings: Nine Lights That Govern the Chart
Each Graha and Its Domain
Roots in the Vedic Sky
Parashara and the Science of Reading Them
Shrines Where the Nine Stand Together
Stotras Recited for the Grahas
essay: Walk into almost any South Indian temple and you will find a low platform where nine small figures face outward in different directions, never quite looking at one another. These are the Navagraha, the nine grahas of Vedic jyotisha. The word graha means "the one who seizes" or grips, and that is the whole idea. Their positions at the moment of birth are read as forces that pull on a life. Devotees light ghee lamps before them not to flatter but to make peace with influences already in motion.

The nine are not abstract. Surya is the Sun, the soul and the father; Chandra the Moon, mind and mother. Mangala, ruddy Mars, carries energy and quarrel. Budha, Mercury, governs speech and reckoning. Brihaspati, Jupiter, is the great teacher of the Devas and the planet of wisdom. Shukra, Venus, rules pleasure and the arts. Slow Shani, Saturn, weighs every deed. Then come the two shadow grahas with no body of their own, Rahu and Ketu, the lunar nodes, eclipse-makers blamed for sudden upheaval and odd cravings.

Their story in scripture is older than the neat platform suggests. Surya, Chandra, Brihaspati and Shukra appear throughout the Vedas as living deities long before anyone arranged them into nine. Rahu enters as the demon who stole a sip of the gods' amrita and was beheaded, his severed head and trunk becoming the two nodes. The jyotisha tradition gathered these strands gradually, assigning each graha a metal, a grain, a color, a gemstone and a cardinal direction, until the nine became a single working system.

Most of what astrologers actually do with the grahas traces back to the Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra, the foundational text attributed to the sage Parashara. It lays out the houses, the aspects, the dashas or planetary periods, and the remedial logic that follows from a difficult placement. A heavy Shani transit, an afflicted Mangala, a Rahu-Ketu axis cutting across the chart, each calls for its own response. Mantra, fasting on the graha's weekday, charity in its color, the wearing of its stone, all flow from reading the nine.

The most famous shrine is at Suryanarayana and the great Navagraha temples of Tamil Nadu, where pilgrims walk a circuit of nine separate temples, one for each graha, scattered across the Kumbakonam region. Closer to home, the standard temple platform keeps Surya at the center facing east, the others ranged around him, each turned so that no two grahas exchange a direct gaze, a deliberate arrangement meant to keep their conflicting natures from colliding. Worshippers circle the platform, offering oil and prostration to whichever graha troubles their chart.

When a devotee wants to address all nine at once, the recitation of choice is the Navagraha Stotra traditionally ascribed to Vyasa, which honors each graha in a single verse of its own, from Surya down to Ketu. For protection, the Navagraha Kavacham wraps the worshipper in the armor of all nine. The Navagraha Gayatri offers a separate Gayatri-form invocation for each planet. Beyond these, jyotisha priests draw on individual mantras such as the bija syllables and the planetary peace rites known as graha shanti.

essayHeadings: The Thunder-Wielding King
How the Rigveda Sang of Him
Vritra and the Release of the Waters
Lord of Svarga and the Rains
A King Who Could Be Humbled
Hymns and Stotras to Indra
essay: No god in the oldest Indian scripture is praised more often than Indra. He drinks the soma, mounts the storm, and hurls the vajra, a thunderbolt forged from a sage's bones. Around a quarter of the Rigveda's hymns are addressed to him, more than to any other deity except Agni, his close rival in the count. He is the king of the Devas, broad-shouldered and impatient, and the early seers loved him precisely because he fought.

The hymns do not describe a serene ruler. They describe a warrior who roars, who is exhorted to drink before battle, whose belly the poets compare to a lake of soma. Indra rides in a golden chariot drawn by tawny horses called the Haris. His weapon is the vajra fashioned by Tvashtr or, in another telling, from the spine of the rishi Dadhichi. The Rigvedic poets call on him for cattle, for victory, for the strength to break open whatever holds back what their people need.

His defining deed is the slaying of Vritra. The serpent-demon Vritra had coiled around the mountains and swallowed the waters of the world, leaving the rivers dry. Indra, fortified with soma, struck him with the vajra and split him open, and the seven rivers ran free. The Rigveda returns to this combat again and again. It is read both as a literal storm-myth, the thunderbolt cracking the cloud to loose the rain, and as the cosmic act that set the waters and the world in motion.

From that victory comes his standing as lord of rain and of Svarga, the bright heaven where the righteous and the gods feast. Indra commands the monsoon that a farming people lived or died by, so the hymns plead with him to send the clouds and withhold the drought. His court holds the apsaras and the gandharvas; Airavata, the white elephant, is his mount in later lore, and Nandana his celestial garden. To reach Indra's heaven was, for the Vedic mind, the reward of a life well lived.

Later literature is less reverent. The epics and Puranas show Indra anxious about his throne, sending apsaras to break the penances of rishis who grow too powerful, and suffering for his pride. Krishna lifts Mount Govardhan to shelter the cowherds from Indra's furious rain, teaching the king of gods that devotion outranks ritual fear. These stories do not erase his Vedic grandeur so much as humanize it, a once-supreme god learning the limits of supremacy.

Devotees who turn to Indra most often recite the Indra Suktam, the Rigvedic hymn that gathers his epithets and exploits into a single act of praise. The Indra Stotram addresses him as king of the Devas and asks his protection and his rain. The Indrakshi Stotram, framed around the goddess Indrakshi, "she of Indra's eyes," is chanted for healing and the warding of disease and is closely tied to his name. Together these keep alive the oldest object of Vedic song.

essayHeadings: The God You Cannot See
Breath, Prana, and the Living Body
Father of Hanuman and Bhima
An Old Vedic Companion of Indra
Mukhyaprana in the Dvaita Path
Stutis and Stotras of the Wind
essay: You can hear Vayu but never look at him. The wind god has no fixed form, only motion, the rush through the trees and the breath in the chest. The Rigveda calls him swift, harnessing a thousand horses to his chariot, and pairs him with Indra in the cool of morning when the soma is fresh. Of all the elemental deities he is the most intimate, because the same air he commands outside the body is the air that keeps the body alive.

That double meaning is the heart of how the tradition treats him. Vayu is wind in the world and prana, the vital breath, within. The Upanishads identify prana as the force that holds the senses and the life together; when the other faculties quarrel over which is greatest, it is the breath whose departure ends the argument, since without it nothing else can function. So Vayu is not one element among five but the moving principle that animates a person, the link between the cosmos outside and the spirit within.

He is also a father. Hanuman, the mighty vanara devotee of Rama, is Vayuputra, son of the wind, and his leaps across oceans and his unbounded strength come from his father's nature. In the Mahabharata, Bhima, the strongest of the Pandavas, is likewise begotten by Vayu, which is why he can uproot trees and crush enemies with bare hands. Through these two sons the wind's power takes visible shape in the epics, one serving Rama, the other guarding the Pandavas.

In the Veda itself Vayu, sometimes called Vata for the raw blowing wind, is an honored deity who receives the first draught of soma alongside Indra. He is praised as swift-moving, fragrant, the breath of the gods, friend of the waters and herald of the dawn. He is not a major figure in the later popular pantheon, yet he never disappears, because every fire offering needs his breath and every chant needs the air that carries it. The elemental gods endure quietly in ritual.

Where Vayu rises highest is in the Dvaita tradition of Madhvacharya. There he is Mukhyaprana, the chief deity below Vishnu, the perfect devotee and the agent through whom God acts in the world. Madhva's followers count three incarnations of Vayu, Hanuman in the Treta age, Bhima in the Dvapara, and Madhvacharya himself in the present, each a flawless servant of Hari. In this theology Vayu is the model of bhakti, the soul wholly turned toward the supreme.

The great devotional text is the Vayu Stuti of Trivikrama Pandita, a Sanskrit hymn praising Vayu in his three forms and recited widely in Madhva communities for strength and protection. The Vayu Suktam, the Rigvedic invocation, addresses the wind god directly for breath and vigor. Worshippers of Hanuman, his son, also approach the wind through the Hanuman Chalisa and the deeds it celebrates, since to honor Vayuputra is to honor the father whose nature he carries.

essayHeadings: The Faqir Under the Neem Tree
Sabka Malik Ek
The Dhuni and Its Sacred Ash
Thursdays at Shirdi
The Satcharitra and a Saint Remembered
Aartis and Stotras of Sai
essay: Sometime in the 1850s a young man with no name and no stated religion arrived in the village of Shirdi in Maharashtra and settled in a crumbling mosque he called Dwarakamayi. Villagers found him sitting under a neem tree, ash-smeared, begging a little food, speaking in riddles. He stayed roughly six decades and never left. This was Sai Baba of Shirdi, and by the time he died in 1918 thousands of Hindus and Muslims alike were calling him a living saint.

His central teaching needed only three Marathi words: Sabka Malik Ek, one God is master of all. He kept a fire burning in a Hindu mosque, read no single scripture as final, and refused to say plainly whether he was Hindu or Muslim, which was exactly the point. To a devotee asking for a creed he offered Shraddha and Saburi instead, faith and patient endurance, the two qualities he said carried a person across every difficulty. He took the same approach to caste, turning none away.

The fire he tended is the dhuni, and it still burns at Shirdi. Sai Baba distributed the ash from it, called udi, to those who came to him, and countless stories in his tradition tell of that ash curing illness, easing childbirth, calming fear. The dhuni was both a literal hearth from which he fed pilgrims and a working symbol, the ego burned away, the impermanence of the body made plain. Udi from the Shirdi dhuni is carried home by visitors to this day.

Thursday is the day of Sai. It falls on Guruvar, the day of the guru in the Hindu week, and Shirdi fills with the heaviest crowds then; many devotees fast, visit the temple, and read his life on that day. He himself began the practice of the palki procession and the regular aartis that still structure worship at the shrine. The Samadhi Mandir at Shirdi, built over his tomb, draws pilgrims in such numbers that it ranks among the most-visited religious sites in India.

Almost everything known of his life and sayings comes through the Shri Sai Satcharitra, composed in Marathi verse by Govind Raghunath Dabholkar, whom Sai Baba himself nicknamed Hemadpant. Written in the years after the saint's death and drawing on the memories of those who knew him, it records the leelas, the miracles and parables, that devotees read chapter by chapter, often completing the book in a seven-day saptah. It is to Sai devotion what a gospel is, the source text from which the practice flows.

Daily worship at Shirdi turns on the Sai Baba Aartis, four sung across the day, the Kakad Aarti at dawn and the Shej Aarti at night among them, composed largely by his early devotee Das Ganu and others. The Shri Sai Satcharitra itself is recited as devotion. The Sai Chalisa, forty verses in praise of Baba, is chanted by households far from Shirdi, and many keep a simple daily reading of his eleven sayings, the assurances he is believed to have given his devotees.

essayHeadings: The First to Die
Dharmaraja and the Ledger of Deeds
Son of the Sun, Lord of the South
What Nachiketa Asked Him
The Messengers and the Buffalo Mount
Stotras Recited Before Yama
essay: Long before he was the dreaded judge of the dead, Yama was simply the first man to die. The Rigveda remembers him as the mortal who went ahead and found the path that all who follow must take, and so became king of the departed. He is not, in that oldest layer, a monster. He is the elder who discovered the road and rules the realm at its end, gathering the ancestors to himself in a place of light.

Out of that role grew Yama as Dharmaraja, the king of dharma, who judges each soul by the strict account of its deeds. His scribe Chitragupta is said to keep the ledger in which every act, good and ill, is written down, and at death the balance is read. Yama is severe but not cruel; he is justice without favor, which is why he is also called Dharma. To stand before him is to meet the consequence of a life exactly as it was lived, neither softened nor exaggerated.

His lineage ties him to the sky. Yama is the son of Surya, the sun, and the brother of Yami, who in some hymns becomes the river Yamuna, and of Shani in later genealogy. He is the lokapala, the guardian, of the south, the direction the dead are said to travel, and his city Yamapuri or Samyamani lies that way. Temples orient his shrine to the south, and the southward turn carries the association of death in much of Hindu ritual practice for this reason.

The deepest treatment of Yama is the Katha Upanishad, where the boy Nachiketa, given to Yama by a careless father, waits three days at his door. Yama, absent, owes him three boons in recompense, and with the third Nachiketa asks the one thing Yama would rather not teach, what happens after death, whether the Self survives. Yama tries to deflect him with wealth and long life, but the boy refuses, and so the god of death delivers one of the great teachings on the immortal Self, the Atman that is never born and never dies.

Popular imagination paints him in darker colors. Yama rides a black buffalo, carries a noose, the pasha, to draw out the soul, and a mace or danda, his rod of authority. His messengers, the Yamadutas, come to fetch the dying, and devotional tales set them against the messengers of Vishnu who rescue the faithful. Yet even here the meaning holds: the noose is inevitability, the rod is law, and the buffalo's slow tread is the certainty that none outrun.

Those who seek his favor or fearlessness at death recite the Yama Stotram and the Yamashtakam, eight verses honoring Dharmaraja as the just lord of the south. The Katha Upanishad is read not as a stotra but as the very words of Yama on the deathless Self, studied for the freedom it promises. On Yama Dwitiya during the festival of Bhai Dooj, and in the lamp-offering of Yama Deepam at Diwali, households invoke him directly, asking the lord of death to spare untimely ending.

essayHeadings: The Woman Who Argued With Death
A Princess Who Chose a Doomed Husband
The Day Yama Came
Outwitting the Lord of the Dead
Savitri and the Solar Name
Vrats and Stotras in Her Memory
essay: There is a moment in the Mahabharata when a young woman follows the god of death down the road, refusing to turn back, talking the whole way. The woman is Savitri, and she is walking behind Yama because he has just taken the soul of her husband. Her story sits in the Vana Parva, told to the exiled Yudhishthira as the Pativrata Mahatmya, the greatness of the devoted wife, and it is one of the few tales where a mortal bests death by sheer persistence.

Savitri was the daughter of King Ashvapati, born after long penance and named for the solar deity Savitr whom her father had worshipped. Grown and unmarried, she set out herself to find a husband and chose Satyavan, a prince living in forest exile with his blind, dethroned father. The sage Narada warned her: Satyavan was noble, but fated to die in exactly one year. Her parents urged her to choose again. She would not. She had chosen once, she said, and a woman of her word does not choose twice.

She married him and lived the year quietly in the forest. Three days before the end she took a hard vow, fasting and standing, the rite later devotees would imitate. On the fated day she followed Satyavan into the woods as he went to cut wood. There his head began to ache; he lay down with it in her lap, and his breath stopped. Then she saw a figure she had not seen before, a dark lord with a noose, come to draw out her husband's soul. It was Yama himself, come in person for so good a man.

Yama took the soul and started south, and Savitri walked after him. He told her to go home; the living do not follow the dead. She answered with words so wise on dharma that Yama, pleased, offered her a boon, anything but Satyavan's life. She asked sight for her father-in-law, and he granted it. Still she kept walking, kept speaking, kept earning boons. Then, granted sons for herself, she sprang the trap: how could she bear sons without her husband? Cornered by his own word, Yama released Satyavan, and she walked back to wake him.

The name Savitri carries a second resonance. Savitr is the solar aspect of the sun, and Savitri as a feminine form is one of the names of the Gayatri, the great Vedic verse and its goddess, sometimes counted a consort of Brahma. Devotees do distinguish the two: the Gayatri-Savitri belongs to Vedic worship and the daily sandhya, while the heroine of the Mahabharata is the human wife whose story is read for the model of fidelity. The shared name links solar light to a woman who would not let death keep what was hers.

Her memory lives most strongly in the Vat Savitri vrat, observed by married women across northern and western India, who fast and circle a banyan, the vat tree, tying thread around its trunk and praying for their husbands' long life. The Savitri Vrat Katha, the story recited during the fast, retells the Mahabharata episode for each year's observance. The Savitri Stotram is chanted in her honor, and in the festival's regional forms, Vat Purnima among them, her argument with Yama is remembered as the wife's victory over death itself.
